description: Lines 221-245 (GenerateAdminToken), 262-321 (ValidateAdminToken), 326-379 (ListAdminTokens), 384-432 (RevokeAdminToken) use type assertions for *database.DB and *database.DatastoreDB. If neither matches, they return generic error. Issue: Error messages don't indicate which DB type was received, making debugging difficult. No compile-time check that all DB types are handled. Fix: Consider interface method for admin tokens in Database interface, or log the actual type in error messages.
title: subscription_service.go:Admin token type assertions don't handle unknown DB types

close_reason: Implemented atomic hit/miss counters in all 3 caches with tests
description:
Enhancement: Add cache hit/miss tracking to measure cache effectiveness.

Locations:
- internal/cache/unread_cache.go
- internal/cache/feed_list_cache.go

Rationale:
- Measure cache effectiveness and hit rates
- Help tune TTL values
- Identify performance issues

Recommended implementation:
1. Add atomic counters for hits and misses
2. Track in Get() method
3. Expose in GetStats()

Example:
type CacheStats struct {
    CachedUsers int
    TotalFeeds  int
    Hits        int64
    Misses      int64
    HitRate     float64  // Calculated: hits / (hits + misses)
}

func (uc *UnreadCache) Get(userID int) (map[int]int, bool) {
    // ... existing logic ...
    if hit {
        atomic.AddInt64(&uc.hits, 1)
    } else {
        atomic.AddInt64(&uc.misses, 1)
    }
    return result, hit
}

Benefits:
- Data-driven performance tuning
- Identify cache warming opportunities
- Monitor production effectiveness

Impact: Enhancement - improves observability
Priority: Low - nice to have for production monitoring
design: Add atomic hit/miss counters to all 3 caches (UnreadCache, FeedListCache, SessionManager). Track in Get()/GetSession() methods. Extend existing GetStats()/GetCacheStats() return types with Hits, Misses, HitRate fields. Update corresponding tests.
title: Add cache hit/miss metrics for monitoring

description:
Stripe webhook endpoint has no rate limiting. Attacker could flood with fake webhook requests.

Location: internal/handlers/payment_handler.go:68-189
Impact: DoS via webhook flooding, log spam

Fix: Add rate limiting middleware specifically for webhook endpoint
title: Missing rate limiting on Stripe webhook

close_reason: Added mutex protection to mockDB in session_test.go to fix data race
description:
The async DeleteSession change (gr-5iz) introduced a data race in TestSessionExpiration.

Problem: mockDB in session_test.go doesn't have mutex protection for its sessions map. When the async DeleteSession goroutine writes to the map while the test reads from it, we get a race detected by -race flag.

Location: internal/auth/session_test.go

Fix needed:
1. Add sync.RWMutex to mockDB struct
2. Add locking in GetSession, CreateSession, DeleteSession, and DeleteExpiredSessions methods

The race is between:
- mockDB.GetSession() reading m.sessions (line 82)
- mockDB.DeleteSession() deleting from m.sessions (line 96)
title: Fix data race in TestSessionExpiration mockDB

acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Cloud Tasks queue created and configured
- [ ] Cron handlers enqueue tasks and return immediately
- [ ] Task handler endpoints process work correctly
- [ ] Task endpoints properly authenticated (X-CloudTasks headers)
- [ ] Feed refresh completes successfully via Cloud Tasks
- [ ] Cleanup completes successfully via Cloud Tasks
- [ ] Error handling and retry logic tested
- [ ] App Engine instance count reduced for low-traffic periods
- [ ] No 500 errors in production after deployment
description:
Replace synchronous cron job handlers with Cloud Tasks to prevent excessive App Engine instance scaling while ensuring background work completes reliably.

Current problem:
- Cron jobs (feed refresh every 1h, cleanup every 24h) run synchronously
- Long-running operations keep App Engine instances alive
- Causes excessive instance scaling and unnecessary costs for low-traffic deployments
- Previous attempt to use background goroutines failed because App Engine kills goroutines when HTTP response completes

Affected endpoints:
- /cron/refresh-feeds (internal/handlers/feed_handler.go:303)
- /cron/cleanup-orphaned-articles (internal/handlers/feed_handler.go:346)
design:
Use Google Cloud Tasks to process cron work asynchronously:

1. Create Cloud Tasks queue (via Terraform or gcloud)
2. Update cron handlers to:
   - Enqueue task to Cloud Tasks
   - Return 200 OK immediately
   - Task worker endpoint processes the actual work
3. Implement task handler endpoints:
   - /tasks/refresh-feeds (actual feed processing)
   - /tasks/cleanup-orphaned-articles (actual cleanup)
4. Add authentication for task endpoints (verify X-CloudTasks-* headers)
5. Configure task queue with appropriate retry policies

Benefits:
- Cron handlers return immediately (< 100ms)
- App Engine doesn't keep instances running during long operations
- Tasks complete reliably even if instance shuts down
- Proper retry/error handling built into Cloud Tasks
- Reduces instance-hour costs by 30-50% for low-traffic deployments

References:
- https://cloud.google.com/tasks/docs/creating-appengine-handlers
- https://cloud.google.com/appengine/docs/standard/go/taskqueue/push/creating-handlers
notes: 2026-02-21: Partially addressed — the cron handler now spawns a goroutine and returns 202 immediately (commit 27ed7ed). This eliminates the 15-minute instance hold per cron run. However, GAE Standard can kill an instance and its goroutines at any time, so background goroutines are not guaranteed to complete. Cloud Tasks remains the correct long-term solution for guaranteed delivery and retry. The goroutine fix is a low-cost interim improvement; Cloud Tasks would replace it entirely.
title: Implement Cloud Tasks for async cron job processing

description: Currently, feeds in the left sidebar appear to be ordered by title alphabetically. Users should be able to choose how their feeds are ordered.
title: Add user-configurable feed ordering in sidebar

description: Lines 128-139: CleanupOldLimiters removes any limiter at full token capacity (limiter.Tokens() >= burstSize). A limiter can be at full capacity but still actively rate-limiting (e.g., recently used domain that regenerated all tokens). Issue: If cleanup runs between requests, limiter is removed and recreated, resetting rate limit state. Fix: Track last access time per limiter and only cleanup limiters unused for X duration (e.g., 1 hour).
title: rate_limiter.go:CleanupOldLimiters removes in-use limiters

description: Modals lack role=dialog, aria-modal=true, aria-labelledby attributes. No focus trapping implemented - keyboard users can tab behind the modal. Escape key does not close modals. Screen reader and keyboard-only users are significantly affected. File: web/static/js/modals.js lines 21-24, 50-56.
title: Fix modal accessibility (role, aria, focus trap, Esc key)

description:
**Location:** internal/handlers/auth_handler.go - Callback()

**Issue:** Sessions are not regenerated after successful authentication, allowing session fixation attacks. An attacker can set a victim's session ID before login, then hijack the authenticated session.

**Missing:** Session ID regeneration after authentication

**Risk:** LOW - Requires attacker to set victim's session cookie before authentication

**Recommendation:**
1. Delete old session after successful OAuth callback
2. Generate new session with new ID
3. Update session cookie with new ID
title: LOW: Session Fixation Vulnerability on Login

description:
**Location:** Privacy policy referenced but no data export/deletion

**Issue:** The application references a privacy policy but lacks required GDPR compliance features:
- No user data export functionality
- No user account deletion endpoint  
- No data retention policies implemented
- User data (articles read/starred) persists indefinitely

**Current State:**
- Privacy policy page exists at /privacy
- Users can view their data but cannot export it
- No way to delete account and associated data

**Risk:** LOW - Potential GDPR/privacy regulation violations in EU

**Recommendation:**
1. Add 'Export My Data' endpoint (JSON format)
2. Add 'Delete My Account' endpoint (with confirmation)
3. Implement data retention policies
4. Add consent tracking for analytics/cookies
5. Document data processing in privacy policy
title: LOW: Missing GDPR Compliance Mechanisms

description:
**Location:** main.go - Missing security header middleware

**Issue:** The application does not set recommended security HTTP headers:
- X-Frame-Options (clickjacking protection)
- X-Content-Type-Options (MIME sniffing protection)  
- X-XSS-Protection (XSS filter)
- Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS)
- Content-Security-Policy (CSP)
- Referrer-Policy (referrer leakage)

**Risk:** LOW - Missing defense-in-depth layers

**Recommendation:**
Add security headers middleware:
```go
r.Use(func(c *gin.Context) {
    c.Header("X-Frame-Options", "DENY")
    c.Header("X-Content-Type-Options", "nosniff")
    c.Header("X-XSS-Protection", "1; mode=block")
    c.Header("Referrer-Policy", "strict-origin-when-cross-origin")
    if isProduction {
        c.Header("Strict-Transport-Security", "max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains")
    }
    c.Next()
})
```
title: LOW: No Security Headers Configured

Fix: Add created_at to the /auth/me endpoint response.

Recommended implementation:

Impact: Very low - this is mostly a code quality improvement.
title: Improve session expiration check to avoid race condition

description:
feed_service.go:389-390 — MarkUserArticleRead calls unreadCache.Invalidate(userID) after every mark-read action. This throws away the entire unread count cache for the user, so the very next page load triggers GetUserUnreadCounts → N+1 Datastore query pattern (one goroutine per feed, each doing a keys-only query + GetMulti chunk reads).

unread_cache.go:87 has UpdateCount(userID, feedID, wasRead, nowRead) specifically designed for incremental cache updates, but it is never called from the service layer.

Fix: Extend the MarkRead API request body to include feedID and wasRead (the previous read state). The client already has both values — it is displaying the article inside a specific feed. Then call UpdateCount instead of Invalidate.

This converts a cache-busting O(N*M) Datastore operation into an O(1) in-memory map update on every article read action. Given that reading articles is the primary user action, this is the highest-frequency optimization available.
title: Use UpdateCount instead of Invalidate in MarkUserArticleRead

description: Feed handler returns detailed SSRF errors like hostname resolves to blocked IP, leaking infrastructure info. - Scroll position restoration

2. Article list (main performance concern)

- Ensure smooth 60fps scrolling
- Verify keyboard navigation still works
- Check mobile scroll behavior

Files to modify:

- web/static/css/styles.css (virtual list container)

description: Lines 126-155 create HTTP client with hardcoded timeout values (30s dial, 10s TLS, 10s headers, 1s expect-continue). These aren't configurable and may not suit all feed sources. description:

Improvements:

1. Folder/Category support:

2. Visual organization:

3. Folder operations:

4. Import/Export:

5. UI/UX:

The section should cover:
1. How to detect if running as a supervisor (CAO_TERMINAL_ID environment variable)
2. Supervisor responsibilities: coordinate, never write code directly
3. Developer agent: receives tasks via beads issue references, implements code
4. Reviewer agent: reviews code, provides feedback

- RATE_LIMIT_BURST_SIZE (used in config.go:81)

Impact:
Users will see false warnings like 'WARNING: Environment variable RATE_LIMIT_REQUESTS_PER_MINUTE may not be handled' even though it is handled.

Fix:
Add all missing variables to the handledVars map.

Priority: Medium - causes confusion but doesn't break functionality

description:
Enhancement: Make cache cleanup goroutines context-aware for graceful application shutdown.

Locations:
- internal/cache/unread_cache.go
- internal/cache/feed_list_cache.go

Current implementation:
- Cleanup goroutines run indefinitely
- No graceful shutdown mechanism
- Could cause issues during deployment/restart

Recommended implementation:
1. Add Start(ctx context.Context) method
2. Use context for cleanup goroutine lifecycle
3. Support graceful shutdown

Example:
func (uc *UnreadCache) Start(ctx context.Context) {
    go uc.cleanupExpiredEntries(ctx)
}

func (uc *UnreadCache) cleanupExpiredEntries(ctx context.Context) {
    ticker := time.NewTicker(5 * time.Minute)
    defer ticker.Stop()
    
    for {
        select {
        case <-ticker.C:
            // ... cleanup logic
        case <-ctx.Done():
            return  // Graceful shutdown
        }
    }
}

Benefits:
- Cleaner shutdown behavior
- Better testing support
- More production-ready

Impact: Enhancement - improves production readiness
Priority: Low - nice to have for production deployments
title: Add context-aware cleanup for graceful shutdown

description:

description: payment_handler.go lines 35-39 builds redirect URLs using c.Request.Host which can be spoofed via Host header. Should use the pre-configured GOOGLE_REDIRECT_URL environment variable or a fixed base URL instead. File: internal/handlers/payment_handler.go lines 35-39.
title: Fix Host header usage in payment redirect URL construction

description:
datastore.go:1404-1424 — getFeedUnreadCountForUser retries with a 500ms sleep if a feed returns 0 articles on the first attempt, to handle eventual consistency lag. However, 0 articles is a valid result for a new or empty feed, and the 500ms sleep burns instance time on every cold cache hit for every such feed.

Since feeds with 0 articles are legitimate, the retry loop just adds latency. The function is called in parallel goroutines (one per user feed), so even one feed with 0 articles adds 500ms to the total GetUserUnreadCounts duration.

Fix: Remove the retry/sleep logic. Return 0 immediately for feeds with no articles. If eventual consistency is a concern after adding a new feed, handle it at a higher level (e.g., the feed list cache already handles freshness).
title: Remove retry+sleep in getFeedUnreadCountForUser

description:
GetSessionFromRequest is called in every auth middleware (RequireAuth, RequireAdmin, OptionalAuth) without caching. Multiple DB/cache lookups per request.

Location: internal/auth/middleware.go:28, 61, 72
Impact: Unnecessary session store lookups, performance impact

Fix: Cache session in request context on first lookup
title: Session looked up multiple times per request
Add sanity checking for duration config values

description: Current 'make test' runs test.sh which always uses -coverprofile, disabling Go's built-in test cache. This means every 'make' invocation re-runs the full test suite (~70s) even when nothing changed. Add a 'test-quick' target that runs 'go test ./...' without -v or -coverprofile, allowing Go's cache to skip unchanged packages (sub-second when cached). Keep the full 'test' target for CI/pre-deploy. Update 'make all' to use test-quick for faster dev iteration. File: Makefile, test.sh.
title: Add make test-quick target that leverages Go test caching

close_reason: Implemented FilterExistingArticleURLs: single projection query per feed replaces N per-article queries
description:
AddArticle issues one keys-only query per article to check whether a URL already exists before writing. For a feed with 20 articles, that's 20 separate Datastore read operations every cron cycle, even for articles that already exist.

Replace the per-article query with a single batch check per feed refresh: before the save loop, query for all existing article URLs in the incoming batch at once, build an in-memory set of known URLs, then skip articles that are already present without hitting Datastore.

Location: internal/database/datastore.go:AddArticle (line ~367) and the caller internal/services/feed_service.go:saveArticlesFromFeedWithLimit (~line 711).

Approach:
- Add a new method BatchCheckArticleURLs(urls []string) (map[string]bool, error) that queries Article entities filtered by URL using GetAll with a keys-only query
- Call it once per feed in saveArticlesFromFeedWithLimit before the save loop
- Pass the known-URL set into AddArticle (or restructure so AddArticle skips the query when the set is provided)
- Fall back to the current per-article query when called from other callers (e.g. feed add with limit)

This reduces Datastore reads from N per feed to 1 per feed for the common case where all articles already exist.
title: Batch article dedup check in AddArticle to reduce Datastore reads

description: Star button has only 4px padding (styles.css:592), delete feed button is a tiny x character (line 973), filter radio buttons have 6px gap. All below the 48px minimum for comfortable touch interaction. File: web/static/css/styles.css lines 592, 973, 354.

description:
datastore.go:1313-1380 — GetAccountStats fires the same per-feed parallel goroutine pattern as GetUserUnreadCounts, running its own article key queries to calculate total_unread. It does not use the unread cache at all.

When a user visits the account/stats page after already loading their feed list (which populates the unread cache), GetAccountStats re-runs the expensive N+1 query anyway.

Fix: Have GetAccountStats (or its service-layer wrapper) check the unread cache first and sum up the cached counts for total_unread, avoiding the Datastore queries entirely on cache hit.
title: GetAccountStats should use cached unread counts

close_reason: Implemented UpdateFeedAfterRefresh: single Get+Put replaces 2-3 separate writes on feed refresh success path
description:
For each feed checked during a cron refresh cycle, the code makes 2-3 separate Datastore write operations:
1. UpdateFeedTracking (read + write: last_checked, last_had_new_content, average_update_interval)
2. UpdateFeedLastFetch (read + write: last_fetch)
3. UpdateFeedCacheHeaders (read + write: etag, last_modified) — conditional, only when feed returns ETag

Each of these does its own client.Get + client.Put round trip. For 50 feeds that's up to 150 Datastore write operations per cron run, where 50 would suffice.

Consolidate into a single UpdateFeedAfterRefresh(feed Feed) method that writes all changed tracking fields in one Get+Put pair per feed.

Location: internal/database/datastore.go (UpdateFeedTracking ~line 198, UpdateFeedLastFetch ~line 476, UpdateFeedCacheHeaders ~line 222) and the caller internal/services/feed_service.go:updateSingleFeed (~line ~860).

Acceptance criteria:
- All six fields (last_checked, last_had_new_content, average_update_interval, last_fetch, etag, last_modified) written in a single client.Put per feed
- Existing UpdateFeedTracking/UpdateFeedLastFetch/UpdateFeedCacheHeaders methods retained or updated for backward compatibility with other callers
- Tests updated to reflect new call structure
title: Merge per-feed tracking writes into a single UpdateFeed call

description:
RequireAuth middleware only validates session, doesn't check CSRF token. State-changing operations could be vulnerable to CSRF.

Location: internal/auth/middleware.go:26-38
Impact: CSRF attacks possible if CSRF middleware not applied to all routes

Fix: Add optional CSRF validation or document that CSRF middleware must be applied separately
title: Missing CSRF validation in auth middleware

description:
The 'LIMIT ? + 1' pattern for pagination is hardcoded in multiple places.

Location: internal/database/schema.go:747 and elsewhere
Impact: Code duplication, harder to maintain

Fix: Extract to a constant or helper function
title: Hardcoded pagination limit logic

description:
No bounds checking on maxArticles parameter. Could set negative or unreasonably large values.

Location: internal/database/datastore_update.go:10-32, internal/database/schema.go:1313-1329
Impact: Could allow invalid configuration values

Fix: Add validation (e.g., 0 < maxArticles <= 10000)
title: Missing validation in UpdateUserMaxArticlesOnFeedAdd

description:
stripHTMLTags uses simple character-by-character parsing. Malformed HTML with unclosed tags could leave inTag=true and strip all content.

Location: internal/services/feed_service.go:1200-1226
Impact: Data loss, articles with no content if malformed HTML

Fix: Use proper HTML parser like golang.org/x/net/html
title: HTML tag stripper is vulnerable to malformed HTML

description:
GetStats() currently counts expired entries as if they were valid, giving misleading metrics.

Locations:
- internal/cache/unread_cache.go:125-138
- internal/cache/feed_list_cache.go:80-88

Problem:
- Stats include expired but not-yet-cleaned entries
- Misleading for monitoring cache effectiveness

Recommended fix for UnreadCache:
func (uc *UnreadCache) GetStats() CacheStats {
    uc.mu.RLock()
    defer uc.mu.RUnlock()

    now := time.Now()
    activeUsers := 0
    totalFeeds := 0
    
    for userID, counts := range uc.counts {
        if now.Before(uc.refreshAt[userID]) { // Only count valid entries
            activeUsers++
            totalFeeds += len(counts)
        }
    }

    return CacheStats{
        CachedUsers: activeUsers,
        TotalFeeds:  totalFeeds,
    }
}

Similar fix needed for FeedListCache.

Impact: Minor - improves observability accuracy
Priority: Medium - fix after critical memory leaks
title: Filter expired entries from cache stats

description: Lines 56-60 log the first and last 4 characters of the Stripe secret key for debugging. This is a security risk as it leaks partial credentials to logs. Issue: 'Using Stripe key: sk_t...xyz1 (107 chars total)' exposes key prefix/suffix. Fix: Remove credential logging entirely or use a non-reversible hash for diagnostics. In production environments, logs may be stored/transmitted insecurely.
title: payment_service.go:NewPaymentService logs partial Stripe secret key

description: feed_title is available in article data but only shown in article detail view, not in the list. Users cannot tell which feed an article came from without opening it when viewing All Articles. File: web/static/js/app.js lines 1316-1327, line 1499.
title: Show feed source in All Articles list view

description:
Enhancement: Add configurable maximum cache size limits to both caches to prevent unbounded memory growth.

Locations:
- internal/cache/unread_cache.go
- internal/cache/feed_list_cache.go

Rationale:
- Even with cleanup goroutines, active users could cause excessive memory use
- Provides hard limits on memory consumption
- Useful for capacity planning

Recommended implementation:
1. Add maxUsers field to UnreadCache (e.g., 10000)
2. In Set(), check if limit reached
3. If at limit and adding new user, evict oldest entry
4. Consider LRU eviction strategy for better cache hit rates

Example:
type UnreadCache struct {
    counts    map[int]map[int]int
    refreshAt map[int]time.Time
    mu        sync.RWMutex
    ttl       time.Duration
    maxUsers  int // Configurable limit
}

Benefits:
- Predictable memory usage
- Prevents pathological cases
- Better for capacity planning

Impact: Enhancement - nice to have but not critical
Priority: Low - implement after fixing memory leaks
title: Add max cache size limits to prevent unbounded growth
